title: Don’t Build Agents. Build Skills Instead
author: Ishank choudhary
text:
Don’t Build Agents. Build Skills Instead
After watching too many “AI agents” collapse in production, I finally understand why modular skills are the smarter path
Over the past year, I’ve built (and rebuilt) more AI agents than I’d like to admit.
At first, it felt powerful. Give the model tools. Add memory. Wrap it in a loop. Call it an “agent.”
And for demos? It worked beautifully.
But the moment I pushed those agents into messy, real-world workflows — things like onboarding flows, internal ops automation, or domain-heavy enterprise tasks — the cracks started to show.
The model was intelligent.
The architecture looked clean.
But execution was inconsistent.
That’s when a simple idea shifted my thinking:
“Maybe we shouldn’t be building bigger agents.
Maybe we should be building skills.”
This blog is about that shift.
The Real Problem With “General Agents”
We keep expecting AI agents to behave like experienced teammates.
But they’re not.
They’re brilliant at reasoning, pattern recognition, and language. What they lack is procedural muscle memory.
When you ask a general agent to:
- process an internal finance workflow
- deploy a production service correctly
- follow company-specific compliance rules
…it doesn’t “know” those things. It improvises.
And improvisation in structured workflows leads to:
- inconsistent outputs
- missed edge cases
- constant prompt tuning
- brittle reliability
We’ve been trying to solve this by:
- adding more tools
- expanding system prompts
- writing longer instructions
But that doesn’t solve the root issue: lack of packaged domain knowledge.
What Is a Skill (In Developer Terms)?
Think of a skill as a folder that contains procedural intelligence.
Not just a function.
Not just a tool.
But an organized set of:
- instructions
- scripts
- configurations
- documentation
- supporting files
All packaged together.
Instead of telling the agent how to do something every time, you give it a reusable capability.
That’s the difference.
An agent is a reasoning loop.
A skill is applied expertise.
Why Skills Feel So Obvious (Once You See It)
Here’s the mental shift that made it click for me.
In traditional computing:
- The CPU doesn’t “know” how to edit photos.
- The operating system doesn’t “know” how to design websites.
Applications do.
Models are like processors.
The agent runtime is like the OS.
Skills are like applications.
We’ve been trying to stuff application logic directly into the processor.
That’s backwards.
Simplicity Is the Hidden Superpower
One thing I love about the skills concept is how simple it is.
A skill can literally be:
- a folder
- versioned with Git
- shared in a drive
- imported into another system
No special registry required.
That means:
- developers can create them
- non-technical teams can create them
- organizations can maintain internal skill libraries
You don’t need a complex orchestration layer just to define a reusable capability.
It uses primitives we already understand: files, directories, version control.
That’s a big deal.
Skills Are Better Than “Tools”
At first glance, skills sound like tools. But they’re more powerful.
A traditional tool might look like:
send_email(to, subject, body)
But it doesn’t know:
- your company tone
- formatting standards
- approval steps
- logging requirements
A skill can include:
- templating logic
- compliance checks
- custom scripts
- workflow steps
And because it lives in the file system, it’s self-documenting and editable.
Instead of giving an agent a hammer and hoping it swings correctly, you give it a complete blueprint for how to build something.
The Context Window Problem (And Why Skills Solve It)
One of the biggest hidden constraints in agent systems is context.
If you preload everything the agent might ever need, you:
- blow up the context window
- increase latency
- reduce clarity
Skills solve this with something elegant: progressive disclosure.
The agent only sees:
- lightweight metadata about available skills
When it decides to use one:
- it loads the full instructions and files
This keeps the core loop lean and composable.
And it mirrors how humans work.
You don’t carry every manual in your head. You open one when you need it.
A Simple Example: Building a Deployment Skill
Let’s make this concrete.
Imagine you want an agent to deploy a service following company best practices.
Instead of writing a massive system prompt, you create:
/deploy-service-skill/
- instructions.md
- deploy.sh
- checklist.md
- config-template.yaml
Inside instructions.md
, you document:
- required environment variables
- order of steps
- validation rules
Your agent:
- Sees the “Deploy Service” skill metadata.
- Chooses to use it.
- Loads the folder.
- Executes steps using the packaged scripts.
Now deployment logic is:
- versioned
- reusable
- testable
- transferable
That’s a skill.
Why This Scales Better in Organizations
Here’s where this gets really interesting.
In a company, knowledge lives in:
- senior engineers’ heads
- scattered docs
- tribal memory
Skills turn that into:
- portable, executable knowledge
New agents don’t start from zero.
They inherit:
- your best practices
- your workflows
- your guardrails
Over time, this becomes a living knowledge base.
Instead of retraining people repeatedly, you evolve skills.
The Future: Agents Creating Skills
This is where things get exciting.
Once agents can:
- recognize repeated patterns
- package workflows
- generate reusable procedures
They can start creating their own skills.
Imagine:
- An agent completes a complex analysis.
- It notices repetition.
- It packages the workflow as a reusable skill.
- The next agent can use it instantly.
That’s compounding intelligence.
Not just reasoning.
Accumulated capability.
Why This Changes How I Build AI Systems
After internalizing this idea, my approach shifted.
Instead of asking:
“How do I make this agent smarter?”
I now ask:
“What skill is missing?”
This leads to:
- smaller agents
- clearer responsibilities
- reusable knowledge
- easier debugging
It feels less like crafting prompts and more like building software again.
And as developers, that’s where we’re strongest.
Skills Fit Into a Clean Architecture
A healthy agent stack now looks like:
- Model → raw reasoning
- Agent loop → context management
- Runtime → file system & execution
- External connectors → APIs & integrations
- Skills → domain expertise
Each layer has a clear role.
That separation is what makes systems maintainable.
Key Takeaways
- General agents lack domain-specific execution consistency
- Skills package procedural knowledge into reusable units
- Skills are simple: just folders of structured capability
- Progressive loading protects context windows
- Organizations can build evolving skill libraries
- The future includes agents that create and refine their own skills
Final Thought
We don’t need bigger agents.
We need better skills.
The intelligence is already there. What’s missing is structure, memory, and portability of knowledge.
When we treat skills like applications in the AI stack, we unlock something powerful:
Not just smarter agents.
But systems that actually improve over time.
Let’s Discuss
- Have your agents struggled with domain-specific workflows?
- Are you embedding too much logic directly into prompts?
- What’s one workflow in your team that should be a reusable skill?
Curious how others are approaching this shift.
